disease.name: Hyperparathyroidism
disease.note: Excess PTH secretion causing hypercalcemia. Primary (parathyroid adenoma/hyperplasia) is most common. 'Stones, bones, groans, psychiatric overtones' summarizes the multiorgan effects. In Kenya, many cases are detected incidentally on routine lab work. Secondary hyperparathyroidism from chronic kidney disease and vitamin D deficiency is common in our setting. Parathyroidectomy is curative for primary disease. Intraoperative PTH monitoring confirms biochemical cure.
presentingComplaints: Incidental finding of high calcium on blood test
Bone pain or fractures from minor trauma

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Hypercalcemic crisis/renal failure/severe osteoporosis -> SEVERE; mild asymptomatic hypercalcemia -> NON-SEVERE

history_questions.question: Have you ever been told your calcium level is high?
Have you had kidney stones — how many times and how were they treated?
Do you have bone pain or have you had any fractures?
Have you been feeling unusually tired or depressed?
Do you have abdominal pain or persistent constipation?
Are you drinking more water than usual and urinating frequently?
Do you have muscle weakness or aches?
Have you lost weight unintentionally?
Do you have a history of pancreatitis?
Do you have high blood pressure?
Have you ever had a DEXA bone density scan?
Are you on any calcium or Vitamin D supplements?
Do you take lithium for any psychiatric condition?
Do you have a family history of hyperparathyroidism or MEN syndrome?
Do you have a family history of thyroid cancer or other endocrine tumors?
Have you had neck surgery before?
Do you have kidney disease or have you been on dialysis?
Do you have any other medical conditions (sarcoidosis, TB)?
Are you postmenopausal and how long?
Have you noticed any neck lump?

examination_findings.finding: Neck palpation — parathyroid mass rarely palpable (<5%)
Signs of dehydration (dry mucosa, reduced skin turgor)
Band keratopathy (calcium deposits on cornea — slit lamp exam)
Bone tenderness on palpation of long bones or spine
Proximal muscle weakness (difficulty standing from squat)
ECG changes: shortened QT interval, prolonged PR

bedside_scores.name: Surgical Criteria for Primary Hyperparathyroidism (AAES Guidelines)

components.name: Serum calcium >1 mg/dL above upper limit of normal
Bone density T-score <-2.5 at any site

bedside_scores.interpretation: Score >=2: parathyroidectomy indicated. Score 0-1: can be observed if asymptomatic with monitoring. Surgery also indicated for symptomatic disease regardless of score.

subtypes.name: Parathyroid Adenoma (Single Gland Disease)
subtypes.management: Minimally invasive parathyroidectomy (MIP) guided by pre-op localization (sestamibi + US). Intraoperative PTH monitoring (Miami criteria: >50% drop from highest level at 10 minutes post-excision). Four-gland exploration if localization fails or discordant.

subtypes.name: Multigland Hyperplasia (Primary or Secondary)
subtypes.management: Bilateral neck exploration (BNE). Subtotal parathyroidectomy: remove 3.5 glands, leaving well-vascularized remnant (half of one gland). Total parathyroidectomy with autotransplantation (parathyroid tissue into forearm muscle) for MEN1 or renal hyperparathyroidism. Intraoperative PTH monitoring. Thymectomy (may have ectopic parathyroid).

subtypes.name: Parathyroid Carcinoma
subtypes.management: En bloc resection including ipsilateral thyroid lobe, central compartment nodes, and any adherent tissues. No routine lateral neck dissection unless nodes clinically involved. Close follow-up: calcium and PTH every 3 months. Imaging for recurrence. Metastatic disease: palliative surgery, radiotherapy, bisphosphonates.

bedside.name: Corrected calcium calculation
bedside.indication: Correct for albumin: corrected Ca = measured Ca + 0.8 × (40 - albumin)

bedside.name: ECG
bedside.indication: Shortened QT interval, prolonged PR — monitor arrhythmia risk

laboratory.name: Serum Calcium (corrected for albumin)
laboratory.indication: Confirm hypercalcemia — repeat x2 to confirm persistence

laboratory.name: Intact PTH
laboratory.indication: Elevated or inappropriately normal in the presence of hypercalcemia = primary HPT

laboratory.name: 25-OH Vitamin D
laboratory.indication: Rule out secondary HPT from vitamin D deficiency — common in Kenya

laboratory.name: 24-hour Urine Calcium
laboratory.indication: Rule out FHH (low urine calcium); >250mg/day supports primary HPT

laboratory.name: Serum Creatinine, eGFR
laboratory.indication: Assess renal function — surgery indicated if CrCl <60

laboratory.name: Alkaline Phosphatase (bone-specific)
laboratory.indication: Elevated in high bone turnover — indicates bone disease

laboratory.name: PTHrP
laboratory.indication: If malignancy suspected and PTH suppressed

imaging.name: Sestamibi (MIBI) Scan with SPECT/CT
imaging.indication: Gold standard for adenoma localization — shows focus of delayed tracer retention

imaging.name: Neck Ultrasound (high-frequency linear probe)
imaging.indication: Complementary to MIBI — enlarged hypoechoic parathyroid with polar vessel

imaging.name: 4D-CT (CT with perfusion timing)
imaging.indication: Best sensitivity for multigland disease, re-operative cases, when MIBI/US discordant

imaging.name: DEXA scan (bone mineral density)
imaging.indication: Assess osteopenia/osteoporosis at lumbar spine, hip, forearm

resuscitation: For mild hypercalcemia: oral hydration, avoid thiazides and lithium, mobilize
For moderate-severe (Ca >12 or symptomatic): IV Normal Saline 200-300ml/h to correct volume depletion

For hypercalcemic crisis (Ca >14): ICU admission, aggressive IV fluids (4-6L/day), Calcitonin 4-8 IU/kg IM/SC q6-12h, Bisphosphonates (Pamidronate 60-90mg IV or Zoledronic acid 4mg IV), consider dialysis

surgical: Minimally invasive parathyroidectomy (MIP): for single adenoma localized on MIBI + US
Bilateral neck exploration (BNE): for negative/discordant localization, multigland disease, MEN, re-operative surgery
Subtotal parathyroidectomy (3.5 glands): for multigland hyperplasia
Total parathyroidectomy + autotransplantation: for MEN1, renal HPT
En bloc resection: for parathyroid carcinoma
nonOperative: Observation with monitoring: for asymptomatic patients who do not meet surgical criteria
Medical management: adequate hydration, avoid thiazides, Cinacalcet (calcimimetic) for those unfit for surgery

immediate: Monitor calcium (iCa or corrected calcium) at 6h, 12h, 24h, then daily
IV fluids continued until oral intake adequate
Oral calcium supplementation (Calcium carbonate 1-2g TDS) for hungry bone syndrome
Calcitriol 0.25-0.5mcg BD if severe bone disease or symptomatic hypocalcemia
Check PTH at 10-20 min post-excision (intraop) — >50% drop predicts cure
Check PTH and calcium at 6 weeks to confirm biochemical cure
day1_3: Hungry bone syndrome: watch for hypocalcemia (especially if pre-op high ALP, low Vit D, large adenoma)
Symptoms of hypocalcemia: perioral tingling, finger numbness, Chvostek's/Trousseau's
Treat hypocalcemia aggressively: IV Calcium gluconate if symptomatic, oral Calcium + Calcitriol
day3_7: Calcium levels should stabilize by day 5-7
Taper calcium and calcitriol over 2-6 weeks as bones heal
Discharge once calcium stable without IV supplementation

pathways.severity: asymptomatic, mild, not meeting surgical criteria
pathways.criteria: Ca <1mg/dL above ULN, no renal stones, T-score >-2.5, age >50, CrCl >60
pathways.treatment: Observation. Monitor serum Ca + Cr + BMD every 1-2 years. Adequate hydration. Avoid thiazides and lithium. Evaluate for secondary causes (Vit D, renal).

pathways.criteria: Ca >1mg/dL above ULN, renal stones, osteoporosis (T<-2.5), age <50, CrCl <60, symptomatic (bones, stones, groans)
pathways.treatment: Parathyroidectomy. MIP if single adenoma localized. BNE if not. Intraoperative PTH monitoring (>50% drop confirms cure). Post-op calcium monitoring for hungry bone syndrome.

pathways.criteria: Ca >14 mg/dL, confusion, dehydration, arrhythmia, coma
pathways.treatment: Admit to ICU. Aggressive IV fluids (Normal Saline 4-6L/day). Calcitonin 4-8 IU/kg q6-12h. Zoledronic acid 4mg IV. Urgent parathyroidectomy once calcium stable. Dialysis if renal failure.

operative_templates.indication: Single parathyroid adenoma localized on sestamibi and/or ultrasound
operative_templates.approach: Focused, image-guided approach through small incision
operative_templates.position: Supine with neck extended (shoulder roll, head ring)
operative_templates.incision: Small (2-3cm) incision over the localized adenoma or centrally placed and directed to the side of the adenoma
steps: Review pre-op localization images in theatre
Incision centered over the anticipated adenoma location
Divide platysma, retract strap muscles
Identify the adenoma — typically an oval, brownish-tan mass, larger than normal parathyroid (normal ~5x3x1mm)
Carefully dissect adenoma with gentle blunt dissection
Clip the vascular pedicle and divide
Remove adenoma and send for frozen section confirmation
Draw blood for intraoperative PTH at time 0 (before excision) and 5 and 10 minutes after excision
If PTH drops >50% from highest level at 10 min: confirm cure, close incision
If PTH does not drop sufficiently: convert to bilateral neck exploration
keyLandmarks: Recurrent laryngeal nerve — may be in close proximity to adenoma, identify before clipping pedicle
Normal parathyroid glands — identify and protect

operative_templates.indication: Negative or discordant localization, multigland hyperplasia, MEN syndrome, re-operative surgery
operative_templates.approach: Standard collar incision with bilateral exploration
steps: Collar incision, subplatysmal flaps, divide strap muscles in midline
Systematically identify all four parathyroid glands
Normal parathyroid: tan/brown, 3-5mm, flattened oval
Adenoma: larger, darker, rounder, more vascular
Assess gland weight and appearance
For single adenoma: excise as in MIP
For hyperplasia: subtotal (3.5 gland) resection: identify all 4 glands, remove 3 entire glands, leave half of the best-vascularized gland with clip marker
Send largest gland for frozen section to confirm hypercellular parathyroid
Intraoperative PTH: confirm >50% drop at 10 min
Hemostasis, drain if large dissection, close in layers
keyLandmarks: Superior parathyroids: dorsal to upper thyroid pole, near cricoid cartilage
Inferior parathyroids: near lower thyroid pole, within 1cm of inferior thyroid artery, variable position (may be in thymic tongue)
RLN: in tracheoesophageal groove, cross inferior thyroid artery
Thymic tongue: often contains ectopic inferior parathyroid

watchFor: persistent requirement for calcium + calcitriol beyond 6 months

dischargeCriteria: Calcium stable (corrected Ca >2.0 mmol/L or iCa >1.0 mmol/L) without IV supplementation
Hypocalcemia symptoms (if present) well controlled on oral calcium and calcitriol
PTH confirmed curative drop (>50% from pre-op) or pending 6-week confirmatory test
Oral calcium and calcitriol prescriptions (if needed) with clear dosing instructions
Follow-up for 6-week calcium and PTH check scheduled

patient_education.explanation: Hyperparathyroidism means your parathyroid glands (four tiny glands in your neck behind the thyroid) are making too much parathyroid hormone (PTH). This causes your blood calcium to rise, which can lead to kidney stones, bone thinning, fatigue, and other problems. The most common cause is a benign growth (adenoma) on one gland. Surgery to remove the abnormal gland is the only cure and is successful in over 95% of cases.
warningSigns: After surgery: tingling or numbness around the lips, fingers, or toes
Muscle cramps or spasms in the hands or feet (signs of low calcium)
Sudden neck swelling or difficulty breathing
activity: After surgery: gentle walking, avoid heavy lifting for 2 weeks
Return to work in 1-2 weeks for office jobs, 4 weeks for manual labor
Stay active — weight-bearing exercise helps bone density
diet: If you have hungry bone syndrome (low calcium after surgery): increase calcium-rich foods (milk, yoghurt, cheese, leafy greens)
Stay well hydrated — drink at least 2 litres of water daily
medication: If prescribed calcium and calcitriol: take exactly as directed — do not stop suddenly
You will likely need calcium + Vitamin D for 2-6 weeks after surgery
Avoid thiazide diuretics and lithium (they raise calcium)
followUp: Return in 6 weeks for blood tests: calcium, PTH, creatinine, vitamin D
If calcium and PTH are normal — you are cured
Bone density scan (DEXA) after 1-2 years to reassess bone health
Kidney ultrasound if you had stones

before_discharge: Calcium stable and safe for discharge
Oral calcium and calcitriol prescribed if needed

after_discharge.action: Wound check, histology review (adenoma vs hyperplasia vs carcinoma)
Serum calcium, PTH, creatinine, vitamin D — confirm biochemical cure
Clinical review — symptoms, BP, calcium level
Repeat calcium, PTH — if normal, initiate annual surveillance
Annual surveillance: calcium, PTH, creatinine. DEXA scan if osteopenic.
Genetic counseling referral if MEN suspected (young age, family history, multigland)

when_to_return: Tingling or numbness around the lips or fingers not controlled by calcium
Fever with wound redness or discharge
Persistent fatigue, bone pain, or kidney stones (possible recurrence)

pathophysiologyDetail: The calcium-sensing receptor (CaSR) is a G-protein coupled receptor on parathyroid chief cells that detects extracellular calcium — inactivating mutations or acquired loss of CaSR function in adenomas resets the set-point, allowing PTH secretion despite high calcium
Parathyroid adenomas are monoclonal neoplasms: cyclin D1 overexpression (PRAD1 gene rearrangement on chromosome 11q13) drives cell cycle progression in 20-40% of sporadic adenomas

PTH binds to PTH1R (PTH/PTHrP receptor) on osteoblasts, which then secrete RANKL — RANKL binds to RANK on osteoclast precursors, activating NF-κB signaling and increasing osteoclast formation and activity

Increased bone turnover: PTH stimulates osteoclastic bone resorption, releasing calcium and phosphate into circulation. Cortical bone is preferentially resorbed (distal radius), while trabecular bone may be relatively preserved

Renal effects of PTH: (1) increases calcium reabsorption in the distal tubule (via TRPV5/TRPV6 channels), (2) inhibits phosphate reabsorption in the proximal tubule (causing hypophosphatemia), (3) stimulates 1-alpha-hydroxylase to convert 25-OH vitamin D to active 1,25-(OH)2 vitamin D
1,25-(OH)2 vitamin D increases intestinal calcium and phosphate absorption (via TRPV6 and NaPi-IIb transporters), amplifying the hypercalcemic effect

Hypercalcemia causes: shortened QT interval (ECG), nephrogenic diabetes insipidus (polyuria, polydipsia via impaired ADH action), neuromuscular irritability (muscle weakness, hyporeflexia), and CNS effects (confusion, coma in crisis)
Chronic hypercalcemia leads to calcium deposition in renal tubules (nephrocalcinosis), formation of calcium oxalate or calcium phosphate kidney stones, and progressive renal impairment

Osteitis fibrosa cystica is the classic skeletal manifestation of severe, long-standing hyperparathyroidism: subperiosteal bone resorption (radial aspect of middle phalanges — pathognomonic), salt-and-pepper skull, brown tumors (osteoclastomas), and bone cysts

Hungry bone syndrome: after successful parathyroidectomy, the sudden drop in PTH causes rapid bone remineralization — calcium and phosphate are pulled from the bloodstream into bone, causing severe, prolonged hypocalcemia

In Kenya, vitamin D deficiency is common and can mask primary hyperparathyroidism: PTH is elevated but calcium may be normal/near-normal due to concomitant vitamin D deficiency. Correcting vitamin D deficiency 'unmasks' the true hypercalcemia — the so-called 'tertiary' or 'normocalcemic' hyperparathyroidism

Secondary hyperparathyroidism in chronic kidney disease: renal failure causes phosphate retention (stimulates FGF23), reduced 1-alpha-hydroxylase activity (low 1,25-vitamin D), and hypocalcemia — all three drive PTH secretion in an attempt to maintain calcium homeostasis

landmarkTrials.trial: Silverberg SJ, et al. Natural history of primary hyperparathyroidism — a 10-year prospective study
landmarkTrials.population: 121 patients with asymptomatic primary hyperparathyroidism followed without surgery for 10 years
landmarkTrials.finding: Over 10 years, 37% developed surgical indications (worsening hypercalcemia, reduced bone density, kidney stones, or fractures). Disease progression was unpredictable in individuals
landmarkTrials.impact: Established that observation is not 'benign neglect' — a significant minority of asymptomatic patients progress and require surgery
landmarkTrials.reference: Silverberg SJ, et al. N Engl J Med. 1999;341(17):1249-1255

landmarkTrials.trial: Udelsman R, et al. Minimally invasive parathyroidectomy vs bilateral neck exploration
landmarkTrials.population: 1,500 consecutive patients comparing MIP vs BNE for primary hyperparathyroidism
landmarkTrials.finding: MIP: 97% cure rate, 35-minute operative time, 4-hour hospital stay. BNE: 98% cure rate, 75-minute OR time, 24-hour stay
landmarkTrials.impact: MIP became the standard for localized single adenoma — equivalent cure, shorter operation, outpatient surgery possible

landmarkTrials.trial: Norman J, et al. Intraoperative PTH monitoring — the Miami criteria validation
landmarkTrials.population: 1,500 patients undergoing parathyroidectomy with intraoperative PTH monitoring
landmarkTrials.finding: A >50% drop from the highest pre-excision PTH level at 10 minutes post-excision predicted cure with 97% accuracy. The highest PTH level (not pre-incision) is the correct reference
landmarkTrials.impact: Validated the Miami criteria as the standard for intraoperative PTH interpretation, enabling focused surgery

landmarkTrials.trial: Kahn A, et al. Parathyroidectomy vs Medical Management in Mild Primary Hyperparathyroidism (MILD-PHPT)
landmarkTrials.population: 196 patients with mild primary hyperparathyroidism randomized to surgery vs medical observation
landmarkTrials.finding: Parathyroidectomy improved BMD (5% at spine, 3% at hip), quality of life scores, and reduced kidney stone formation. Medical management had stable BMD and no QOL improvement
landmarkTrials.impact: Provided evidence that even 'asymptomatic' patients benefit from parathyroidectomy beyond meeting traditional surgical criteria
landmarkTrials.reference: Kahn A, et al. J Bone Miner Res. 2020;35(3):452-460

landmarkTrials.trial: Wetmore JB, et al. Cinacalcet for secondary hyperparathyroidism in dialysis patients (EVOLVE)
landmarkTrials.population: 3,883 patients on hemodialysis with secondary hyperparathyroidism randomized to Cinacalcet vs placebo
landmarkTrials.finding: Cinacalcet reduced PTH levels (from 693 to 423 pg/mL) and was associated with a trend toward reduced cardiovascular events (HR 0.93, p=0.11)
landmarkTrials.impact: Cinacalcet is an effective medical option for reducing PTH in secondary HPT but did not meet statistical significance for primary cardiovascular endpoint
landmarkTrials.reference: EVOLVE Trial, Chertow GM, et al. N Engl J Med. 2012;367(26):2482-2494

landmarkTrials.trial: Tominaga Y, et al. Total parathyroidectomy with autotransplantation vs subtotal for renal HPT
landmarkTrials.population: 185 patients with secondary hyperparathyroidism randomized to total parathyroidectomy + autotransplant vs subtotal parathyroidectomy
landmarkTrials.finding: Both procedures were equally effective (90% PTH reduction). Recurrence: 10% in total + autotransplant vs 15% in subtotal at 5 years. Graft-dependent recurrence can be managed by removing graft
landmarkTrials.impact: Both approaches are valid for renal HPT; total + autotransplant has the advantage of managing graft recurrence locally
landmarkTrials.reference: Tominaga Y, et al. World J Surg. 2003;27(2):175-182

evidenceTable.recommendation: Parathyroidectomy is indicated for all symptomatic primary hyperparathyroidism (kidney stones, fractures, osteoporosis, hypercalcemic symptoms)
evidenceTable.reference: AAES Guidelines; Silverberg SJ, N Engl J Med 1999

evidenceTable.recommendation: Parathyroidectomy is indicated for asymptomatic patients who meet any AAES surgical criteria (Ca >1mg/dL above ULN, age <50, CrCl <60, T-score <-2.5, nephrolithiasis)
evidenceTable.reference: AAES Guidelines 2022; Kahn A, J Bone Miner Res 2020

evidenceTable.recommendation: Minimally invasive parathyroidectomy with intraoperative PTH monitoring is the gold standard for single adenoma localized on pre-op imaging
evidenceTable.reference: Udelsman R, Surgery 2007; Norman J, Surgery 2003

evidenceTable.recommendation: Sestamibi scan with SPECT/CT and neck ultrasound are complementary localization studies for pre-operative planning
A >50% drop in intraoperative PTH from the highest pre-excision level at 10 minutes post-excision predicts biochemical cure
Bilateral neck exploration is indicated when pre-op localization is negative, discordant, or suggests multigland disease
Subtotal parathyroidectomy (3.5 glands) or total parathyroidectomy + autotransplant are options for multigland hyperplasia
Cinacalcet is an option for patients with severe secondary HPT on dialysis who are unfit for or awaiting surgery
evidenceTable.reference: EVOLVE Trial, Chertow GM, N Engl J Med 2012

evidenceTable.recommendation: Familial hypocalciuric hypercalcemia must be excluded (24h urine calcium, Ca/Cr clearance ratio) before proceeding to parathyroidectomy
Vitamin D deficiency should be corrected before or after parathyroidectomy to prevent severe hungry bone syndrome

disease.diagnosticAlgorithm: Hypercalcemia detected (corrected Ca >10.5 mg/dL or 2.6 mmol/L) → Confirm with repeat measurement → Check intact PTH (iPTH) → PTH elevated or inappropriately normal → Primary hyperparathyroidism (PHPT) likely. PTH suppressed → Non-parathyroid cause (malignancy, sarcoidosis, etc.) → Workup accordingly. → Confirm diagnosis: 24h urine calcium (exclude FHH — low urine Ca with Ca/Cr clearance ratio <0.01). Check serum creatinine, eGFR, 25-OH vitamin D, serum phosphate, ALP. DEXA scan (lumbar spine, hip, distal 1/3 radius). Renal US or non-contrast CT for nephrolithiasis/nephrocalcinosis. → Assess surgical candidacy per AAES criteria: Ca >1mg/dL above ULN? Age <50? CrCl <60? T-score <-2.5? Nephrolithiasis/nephrocalcinosis on imaging? Symptomatic? If YES to any → Surgery indicated. If NO to all → discuss observation vs surgery (MILD-PHPT trial data suggests benefits even in mild disease). → Pre-operative localization: Sestamibi scan with SPECT/CT (day 1 + delayed images). Neck ultrasound (high-frequency linear probe). If discordant or negative → 4D-CT. If CT shows bilateral nodules or normal → consider re-review at expert center. → Surgery: MIP if localized single adenoma (2-3cm focused incision, intraoperative PTH). BNE if negative localization or multigland suspicion. → Post-op: Monitor calcium (6h, 12h, 24h, daily). Oral calcium 1-2g TDS + Calcitriol 0.25-0.5mcg BD if hungry bone syndrome risk. Check PTH at 10 min (cure if >50% drop). Discharge when calcium stable. → Follow-up: 6-week calcium + PTH to confirm cure. Annual calcium + PTH for 2 years, then if normal: no further follow-up. DEXA at 1-2 years.

managementPearls.context: FHH is the most important mimic — do NOT operate
managementPearls.pearl: Familial hypocalciuric hypercalcemia (FHH) is caused by an inactivating CaSR mutation. These patients have lifelong mild hypercalcemia, normal life expectancy, and do NOT benefit from parathyroidectomy. The key test: 24h urine calcium <100mg AND Ca/Cr clearance ratio <0.01. If you do one test in hypercalcemia workup, do the Ca/Cr clearance ratio. Operating on FHH is a disaster — calcium will remain high after 'curative' parathyroidectomy.
managementPearls.evidence: Eastell R, et al. Diagnosis of asymptomatic primary hyperparathyroidism. J Clin Endocrinol Metab 2014

managementPearls.pearl: In Kenya, vitamin D deficiency is common. A patient with vitamin D deficiency and PHPT may have NORMAL serum calcium with elevated PTH. Correct the vitamin D (give 50,000 IU weekly for 8 weeks) — the hypercalcemia will 'unmask' as the vitamin D improves calcium absorption. Do not misdiagnose this as secondary hyperparathyroidism alone.
managementPearls.evidence: Silverberg SJ, et al. Vitamin D and primary hyperparathyroidism. J Bone Miner Res 2007

managementPearls.context: The highest PTH is the reference for ioPTH
managementPearls.pearl: Many surgeons mistakenly use the pre-incision PTH as the baseline for ioPTH monitoring. This is WRONG. PTH often rises with anesthesia induction and gland manipulation. The Miami criteria specify: use the HIGHEST PTH level (pre-incision or pre-excision). A >50% drop at 10 min from this highest level predicts cure. Using a lower baseline can give false-positive cures.

Risk factors: pre-op Ca >12 mg/dL, very high PTH (>500 pg/mL), elevated ALP (bone turnover marker), low vitamin D, large adenoma, and radiographic bone disease. These patients need pre-op vitamin D repletion, post-op aggressive oral calcium (2-3g TDS) + Calcitriol (0.5-1mcg BD). They may need IV calcium for 48-72 hours. Warn them in advance about the tingling.
managementPearls.evidence: Brasier AR, et al. Hungry bone syndrome. Medicine 2004

managementPearls.context: Parathyroid carcinoma — think of it pre-op
managementPearls.pearl: Suspect parathyroid carcinoma when: Ca >14 mg/dL, PTH >5x upper limit, palpable neck mass, and vocal cord palsy. At surgery: look for a grey-white, hard, invasive mass. Do NOT biopsy or crack the capsule. Perform en bloc resection: remove the tumor, ipsilateral thyroid lobe, and central compartment nodes. Parathyroid carcinoma is chemo/radio-resistant — complete resection is the only chance for cure.
managementPearls.evidence: Talat N, et al. Parathyroid carcinoma. Lancet Oncol 2010

managementPearls.pearl: If you cannot find the 'missing' inferior parathyroid, check these ectopic locations in order: (1) thymic tongue (in the anterior mediastinum, reached through the cervical incision), (2) carotid sheath (retract the sternomastoid and look along the carotid), (3) retroesophageal space (palpate behind the esophagus), (4) intrathyroidal (parathyroid within the thyroid — ultrasound may show it pre-op).
managementPearls.evidence: Akerstrom G, et al. Surgical anatomy of human parathyroid glands. Surgery 1984

operativeNuances.pitfall: Incision placed too high or too low — the adenoma may not be accessible through the planned incision
operativeNuances.expertTip: Use the pre-operative sestamibi image to guide incision placement. A common approach: mark the cricoid cartilage and sternal notch. For an inferior gland adenoma, place the incision over the lower 1/3 of the thyroid. For a superior gland adenoma, place it higher. A 2-3cm midline incision can access BOTH sides if conversion to BNE is needed.

operativeNuances.procedure: Identification of the RLN in Parathyroid Surgery
operativeNuances.pitfall: The RLN may be intimately adherent to a large parathyroid adenoma and damaged during gland mobilization
operativeNuances.expertTip: Always identify the RLN BEFORE dissecting the parathyroid adenoma. The nerve is often pushed laterally or stretched over the adenoma. Find the nerve in the tracheoesophageal groove at the level of the lower pole. Trace it superiorly past the adenoma. Only then dissect the adenoma off the nerve. Use a nerve stimulator to confirm.

operativeNuances.procedure: Bilateral Neck Exploration for Multigland Disease
operativeNuances.pitfall: Failing to identify all four glands — the missed gland is usually in an ectopic location, leading to persistent hyperparathyroidism
operativeNuances.expertTip: Systematic exploration: (1) Identify the right superior parathyroid — dorsal to upper thyroid pole at cricoid level. (2) Right inferior — near lower thyroid pole, anterior to RLN. (3) Left superior mirror. (4) Left inferior mirror. If only 3 glands found: open the carotid sheath, explore the thymic tongue, and look retroesophageally. A normal gland is 3-5mm, tan colored. An adenoma is larger, darker, rounder.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Leaving a remnant that is too small (becomes ischemic and causes permanent hypoparathyroidism) or too large (causes persistent disease)
operativeNuances.expertTip: Leave a well-vascularized remnant approximately the size of 1.5 normal glands (50-80mg). The most vascularized gland is usually chosen. Mark the remnant with a permanent clip (for future identification in re-exploration). Confirm viability before closing: the remnant should bleed cut surface within 1-2 minutes. If it does not, choose another gland.

operativeNuances.procedure: En Bloc Resection for Parathyroid Carcinoma
operativeNuances.pitfall: Capsular rupture during mobilization of a parathyroid carcinoma — this seeds the field and causes local recurrence
operativeNuances.expertTip: Do not dissect directly on the tumor capsule. Identify the tumor and stay 2-3mm away from it. Resect en bloc with ipsilateral thyroid lobe. Use diathermy to divide the isthmus. Remove central compartment nodes. If the RLN is invaded by tumor, sacrifice it (the patient will have vocal cord palsy but the alternative is tumor recurrence and certain death).

prognosis.mortality: Primary hyperparathyroidism: normal life expectancy after successful parathyroidectomy. Untreated severe disease carries increased cardiovascular mortality (2-3x) from hypertension, LVH, and arrhythmias. Parathyroid carcinoma: 5-year survival 85% for complete R0 resection, 30% for incomplete resection, <10% for metastatic disease.
prognosis.morbidity: Hungry bone syndrome: 10-20% risk overall, 50-80% in patients with pre-op Ca >12 mg/dL or high ALP. Typically resolves over 2-6 weeks. Persistent hypercalcemia: 2-5% (missed adenoma, multigland disease, ectopic gland). Permanent hypoparathyroidism: 1-2% after MIP, 3-5% after BNE, 5-10% after re-operative surgery. RLN injury: 0.5-1%. Renal function: may stabilize or improve after surgery, but pre-existing nephrocalcinosis is irreversible.
prognosis.recurrence: Single adenoma: 1-3% recurrence at 10 years (from missed second adenoma or multigland disease). Multigland hyperplasia: 10-20% recurrence at 10 years after subtotal parathyroidectomy. MEN1: 30-50% recurrence at 10 years (progressive hyperplasia in the remnant). Renal secondary HPT: 10-15% recurrence after surgery. Parathyroid carcinoma: 50-70% local recurrence at 5 years without en bloc resection; 20-30% with en bloc resection.
prognosis.survival: Normal life expectancy after cure. The skeletal effects (BMD) completely reverse over 2-4 years (cortical bone improves by 10-15%, trabecular by 5-10%). Renal stones stop forming, but existing stones do not dissolve. Neurocognitive symptoms (fatigue, depression) improve in 70-80% of patients. Quality of life returns to population baseline.
prognosis.reference: Silverberg SJ, N Engl J Med 1999; Udelsman R, Surgery 2007; AAES Guidelines 2022
